compliance_guides.title: Restaurant Fire Safety & Hood Suppression Compliance Guide
compliance_guides.summary: Step-by-step guidance to help restaurants meet local fire code, hood suppression, and egress requirements.
compliance_guides.content: This guide explains how restaurants can comply with common fire safety regulations covering cooking equipment, hood and duct suppression, portable extinguishers, emergency lighting, and staff training. It outlines inspection intervals for hood suppression systems, cleaning requirements for ducts, placement of Class K extinguishers near cooking appliances, and spacing of exit signs and emergency lights. A sample inspection checklist is provided, along with documentation tips to prepare for fire marshal visits.

compliance_guides.title: Commercial Kitchen Hood & Fire Suppression Code Checklist
compliance_guides.summary: Detailed checklist for commercial kitchen hood systems, suppression equipment, and interlocks.
compliance_guides.content: Commercial kitchens must comply with stringent codes governing exhaust hoods, ducts, and fire suppression systems. This guide walks through UL 300 and NFPA 96 related requirements in plain language, including nozzle coverage over appliances, manual pull station placement, gas and power shutoff interlocks, and semi-annual inspection scheduling. It also covers documentation of service tags and what to expect during an AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) inspection.

compliance_guides.title: Warehouse Fire Protection & Storage Configuration Guide
compliance_guides.summary: Fire safety guidance for warehouses, including sprinklers, detection, and high-piled storage layouts.
compliance_guides.content: Warehouses face unique risks due to high fuel loads and varying rack configurations. This guide explains how fire alarm and sprinkler design interacts with storage heights and commodities. Topics include detector spacing in open warehouse areas, manual pull station locations at exits, notification appliance placement in noisy environments, and special considerations for high-piled and rack storage. A worksheet is included to document square footage by hazard area.

compliance_guides.title: Office Fire Alarm & Emergency Egress Compliance Guide
compliance_guides.summary: Overview of fire alarm, notification, and egress requirements for office buildings and suites.
compliance_guides.content: This guide covers typical code expectations for office occupancies, including where fire alarm systems are required, minimum device coverage for corridors and open office areas, and spacing for notification appliances. It explains exit sign visibility requirements, emergency lighting illumination levels, and stairwell signage. Templates for occupant emergency plans and annual fire drill records are provided.

compliance_guides.title: Apartment Building Fire & Life Safety Compliance Overview
compliance_guides.summary: Fire and life safety requirements for multi-story apartment buildings, including detection and notification.
compliance_guides.content: Multi-family apartment buildings must balance individual unit protection with building-wide systems. This guide explains requirements for smoke alarms in sleeping rooms and common areas, the difference between single-station detectors and system-connected detectors, and where carbon monoxide detection may be required. It reviews corridor and stairwell notification, fire door signage, and recordkeeping for annual inspections.

articles.title: Smoke vs Heat Detectors: Choosing the Right Protection for Each Room
articles.content:
Smoke and heat detectors respond to different fire conditions, and using the right type in each space improves both safety and reliability.

Smoke detectors (photoelectric or ionization) are best for living spaces, bedrooms, and corridors where smoldering fires need to be detected early. Heat detectors respond to elevated temperatures or rapid temperature rise, making them better suited to kitchens, boiler rooms, garages, and dusty areas where smoke detectors may trigger nuisance alarms.

Key differences:
- Smoke detectors sense particles in the air and provide earlier warning for many common residential fires.
- Heat detectors sense temperature, not smoke, and are less prone to false alarms from cooking or steam.
- Many codes require smoke alarms in sleeping rooms and on every level, while heat detectors are often supplemental.

Placement tips:
- Use listed indoor smoke detectors in bedrooms, hallways, and common living areas.
- Use fixed or rate-of-rise heat detectors in kitchens near (but not directly above) cooking appliances.
- In garages, attics, and mechanical rooms, heat detectors often perform better due to dust, insects, and fumes.

When planning a system, combine both technologies: smoke detectors for early warning in clean areas, and heat detectors in high-nuisance or high-heat locations. Always follow local code requirements and manufacturer spacing recommendations.
articles.excerpt: Understand when to use smoke detectors versus heat detectors so you can reduce false alarms while maintaining reliable fire protection.

articles.title: How to Reduce False Alarms on Wireless Smoke Detectors (WX-200)
articles.content:
Repeated false alarms on wireless smoke detectors are usually caused by environmental conditions or placement issues. This guide focuses on the WX-200 model and similar wireless units.

Common triggers include cooking vapors, steam from showers, dust accumulation, and insects entering the sensing chamber. Start by confirming the detector location: avoid mounting within 10 feet of cooking appliances and keep units away from bathrooms with frequent steam.

For the WX-200, verify the mounting bracket is secure, the battery is fresh and correctly installed, and that the device is properly enrolled with the hub. Use a vacuum with a soft brush to remove dust from the vents and test with the built-in test button instead of real smoke. If nuisance alarms persist, consider relocating the detector a few feet further away from the source of vapors or replacing it with a heat detector in that location (if allowed by code).
articles.excerpt: Troubleshooting checklist for cutting down false alarms on the WX-200 wireless smoke detector.

articles.title: Choosing Fire Extinguishers for Small Businesses
articles.content:
Small businesses often need just a handful of extinguishers, but choosing the right types and sizes is critical.

Most offices, retail shops, and light industrial spaces are best served by ABC dry chemical extinguishers, which handle ordinary combustibles, flammable liquids, and energized electrical equipment. A common choice is a 53 lb or 7 lb unit mounted along normal travel paths.

Commercial kitchens, however, require Class K wet chemical extinguishers near cooking appliances that use oils or fats. These extinguishers are specifically formulated to saponify grease and help prevent re-ignition.

When planning, check local code and insurance requirements for minimum ratings and spacing. Typical recommendations place extinguishers so that occupants never need to travel more than 75 feet to reach one in most light hazard occupancies.
articles.excerpt: Learn which extinguisher types and sizes are appropriate for offices, shops, and commercial kitchens.

articles.title: Exit Sign and Emergency Lighting Code Basics
articles.content:
Exit signs and emergency lighting help occupants find safe egress paths during an emergency. Most codes require internally or externally illuminated exit signs at each exit door and along the path of egress.

LED exit signs are popular for their low energy consumption and long life. Photoluminescent signs can be used in some applications where ambient lighting is sufficient to charge them.

Key points:
- Exit signs must be continuously illuminated when the building is occupied.
- Signs must be visible and not obscured by decorations, banners, or furnishings.
- Emergency lighting must provide a minimum illumination level along egress paths for at least 90 minutes during a power outage.
articles.excerpt: Understand basic code expectations for exit signs and emergency lighting in commercial occupancies.

articles.title: Building a Basic Workplace Fire Safety Kit
articles.content:
A well-rounded workplace fire safety kit goes beyond a single extinguisher. At minimum, most small offices and shops should consider:

- An appropriately sized ABC fire extinguisher placed along primary travel paths.
- Clearly illuminated exit signs above exit doors and at directional changes along the exit route.
- A compact fire blanket near break areas or small kitchenettes where minor cooking tasks occur.

Training staff on where equipment is located and how to use it is just as important as installing the devices themselves. Conduct regular walk-throughs so new employees know where to go and what to look for during an emergency.
articles.excerpt: Three core componentsextinguishers, exit signs, and fire blanketsto improve small workplace safety.

articles.title: Introducing the FireCore 8-Zone Expandable Control Panel
articles.content: Our FireCore FC-8Z-EXP panel is designed for growing facilities that need more flexibility than a simple 2- or 4-zone panel can offer. With support for 8 conventional zones and up to 64 devices, it is ideal for mid-size offices, schools, and mixed-use buildings. Optional network modules allow integration with remote annunciators and building management systems.
articles.excerpt: The new FC-8Z-EXP brings 8-zone flexibility and expansion capability to mid-size fire alarm projects.

articles.title: Case Study: Upgrading a Small Office to a Modern Fire Alarm System
articles.content: A 6-room professional office with a central hallway upgraded from standalone smoke alarms to a code-compliant fire alarm system. The project used a 4-zone FireCore FC-4Z panel, new smoke detectors in each office, and LED exit signs at both corridor exits. The result was improved audibility, centralized supervision, and easier maintenance.
articles.excerpt: How a small office improved safety and code compliance with a 4-zone control panel and new devices.

articles.title: Detector Layout Tips for 3-Story Apartment Buildings
articles.content: Three-story apartment buildings typically require smoke alarms in each sleeping room, outside each sleeping area, and on every level, including basements. Common-area detection and notification may be provided by a centralized fire alarm panel.\n\nUse low-profile smoke detectors in corridors and common rooms, and consider heat detectors in small mechanical rooms or storage areas where smoke detectors would be prone to nuisance alarms.
articles.excerpt: Guidance on placing smoke and heat detectors in typical 3-story multi-family buildings.

articles.title: Fire Detection Strategies for Commercial Kitchens
articles.content: Commercial kitchens are high-risk environments with open flames, hot surfaces, and grease-laden vapors. While the hood and duct suppression system address cooking fires under the hood, additional heat detection can provide early warning and shutdown of gas or electric power.\n\nFixed-temperature or rate-of-rise heat detectors mounted on the ceiling in key locations can help monitor areas not directly covered by the hood system. Class K extinguishers should be placed within easy reach of the cooking line, and staff must be trained in their use.
articles.excerpt: Combine hood suppression, heat detection, and Class K extinguishers for robust commercial kitchen protection.

articles.title: Fire Alarm Maintenance Plans: What Businesses Should Know
articles.content: Routine testing, inspection, and monitoring are essential to keep fire alarm systems reliable. Maintenance plans typically combine central station monitoring with scheduled inspections. A plan that offers 24/7 monitoring and at least two inspections per year is ideal for many businesses that want proactive service and clear documentation for insurers and authorities.
articles.excerpt: Understand how monitoring and inspection frequencies affect system reliability and compliance.
